events.hebrew: מַתְנִי׳ הַכֹּל שׁוֹחֲטִין וּשְׁחִיטָתָן כְּשֵׁרָה, חוּץ מֵחֵרֵשׁ שׁוֹטֶה וְקָטָן, שֶׁמָּא יְקַלְקְלוּ אֶת שְׁחִיטָתָן. וְכוּלָּן שֶׁשָּׁחֲטוּ וַאֲחֵרִים רוֹאִין אוֹתָן – שְׁחִיטָתָן כְּשֵׁרָה.

events.hebrew: אֲמַר לֵיהּ רַב אַחָא בְּרֵיהּ דְּרָבָא לְרַב אָשֵׁי: וְכֹל ״הַכֹּל״ לְכַתְּחִלָּה הוּא? אֶלָּא מֵעַתָּה: הַכֹּל מְמִירִין, אֶחָד הָאֲנָשִׁים וְאֶחָד הַנָּשִׁים – הָכִי נָמֵי דִּלְכַתְּחִלָּה הוּא? וְהָא כְּתִיב: ״לֹא יַחֲלִיפֶנּוּ וְלֹא יָמִיר אֹתוֹ טוֹב בְּרָע אוֹ רַע בְּטוֹב״!

events.hebrew: הָתָם, כִּדְקָתָנֵי טַעְמָא: לֹא שֶׁהָאָדָם רַשַּׁאי לְהָמִיר, אֶלָּא שֶׁאִם הֵמִיר – מוּמָר, וְסוֹפֵג אֶת הָאַרְבָּעִים.

events.hebrew: אֶלָּא הַכֹּל מַעֲרִיכִין וְנֶעֱרָכִין, נוֹדְרִין וְנִידָּרִין, הָכִי נָמֵי דִּלְכַתְּחִלָּה? וְהָא כְּתִיב: ״וְכִי תֶחְדַּל לִנְדֹּר לֹא יִהְיֶה בְךָ חֵטְא״!

Is that an expression indicating it is permitted (Deuteronomy 23:23), indicating that it is preferable not to vow.

events.hebrew: אֲבָל אָמַר ״הֲרֵי עָלַי״ – לֹא.
events.englishLiteral: But says: It upon me no,
events.englishGloss: in the case of one who vows and is incumbent to bring an offering, even Rabbi Yehuda concedes that it is best not to vow at all. Likewise, it is preferable not to vow to donate a certain monetary value to the Temple treasury. Apparently, then, the statements in that mishna: Everyone takes vows of valuation, and: Everyone vows to donate the assessment of a person to the Temple treasury, do not indicate that it is permitted to do so ab initio .

events.hebrew: וּכְתִיב: ״טוֹב אֲשֶׁר לֹא תִדֹּר מִשֶּׁתִּדּוֹר וְלֹא תְשַׁלֵּם״, וְתַנְיָא: טוֹב מִזֶּה וּמִזֶּה שֶׁאֵינוֹ נוֹדֵר כׇּל עִיקָּר, דִּבְרֵי רַבִּי מֵאִיר. רַבִּי יְהוּדָה אוֹמֵר: טוֹב מִזֶּה וּמִזֶּה נוֹדֵר וּמְשַׁלֵּם. וַאֲפִילּוּ רַבִּי יְהוּדָה לָא קָאָמַר אֶלָּא בְּאוֹמֵר ״הֲרֵי זוֹ״,

events.hebrew: מַאי טַעְמָא? אָמַר רַב נַחְמָן בַּר יִצְחָק: דְּמוּת יוֹנָה מָצְאוּ לָהֶן בְּרֹאשׁ הַר גְּרִיזִים, שֶׁהָיוּ עוֹבְדִין אוֹתָהּ. וְרַבִּי מֵאִיר לְטַעְמֵיהּ, דְּחָיֵישׁ לְמִיעוּטָא, וְגָזַר רוּבָּא אַטּוּ מִיעוּטָא. וְרַבָּן גַּמְלִיאֵל וּבֵית דִּינוֹ נָמֵי כְּרַבִּי מֵאִיר סְבִירָא לְהוּ.

events.hebrew: הַלּוֹקֵחַ יַיִן מִבֵּין הַכּוּתִים, אוֹמֵר: ״שְׁנֵי לוּגִּין שֶׁאֲנִי עָתִיד לְהַפְרִישׁ הֲרֵי הֵן תְּרוּמָה, עֲשָׂרָה מַעֲשֵׂר רִאשׁוֹן, תִּשְׁעָה מַעֲשֵׂר שֵׁנִי״, וּמֵיחֵל וְשׁוֹתֶה מִיָּד, דִּבְרֵי רַבִּי מֵאִיר. רַבִּי יְהוּדָה וְרַבִּי יוֹסֵי וְרַבִּי שִׁמְעוֹן אוֹסְרִין.
events.englishLiteral: one who purchases wine from among the Samaritans he says: Two log that I will separate in the future are teruma , ten first tithe; nine second tithe. And he deconsecrates and drink immediately, the statement of Rabbi Meir. Rabbi Yehuda and Rabbi Yosei and Rabbi Shimon prohibit
events.englishGloss: It is taught in a baraita ( Tosefta , Demai 8:7): In the case of just before Shabbat, and presumably teruma and tithes were not separated, he acts as follows: If there are one hundred log of wine in the barrels, as the mandated average measure of teruma is one-fiftieth; log are and a tenth of the remainder, which is log , are the second tithe that he will separate in the future, transferring its sanctity to money, he may the wine relying on the separation that he will perform later, which will clarify retroactively which log he designated for the tithes and for teruma . This is this practice. Apparently, Rabbi Yehuda does not accept the principle of retroactive designation.

events.hebrew: הָתָם כִּדְקָתָנֵי טַעְמָא, אָמְרוּ לוֹ לְרַבִּי מֵאִיר: ״אִי אַתָּה מוֹדֶה שֶׁמָּא יִבָּקַע הַנּוֹד וְנִמְצָא שׁוֹתֶה טְבָלִים לְמַפְרֵעַ?״ אָמַר לָהֶן: ״לִכְשֶׁיִּבָּקַע״.
events.englishLiteral: there, the reason as is taught said to Rabbi Meir: Don’t you concede perhaps the wineskin will burst and will have been found retroactively drinking untithed produce? said to When it bursts,
events.englishGloss: The Gemara comments: That is no proof, as for the opinion of Rabbi Yehuda is in the latter clause of the baraita : The Rabbis that before he manages to separate the teruma , this person to be Rabbi Meir the Rabbis: The mere possibility that this may occur is not a concern. actually I will be concerned. Evidently, Rabbi Yehuda’s opinion is not due to his rejection of the principle of retroactive designation, but due to his concern that the wineskin will burst before the tithes are actually separated.

events.hebrew: וְאָמַר רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן: וּכְבָר בָּא חָכָם.
events.englishLiteral: And Rabbi Yoḥanan said: where the Sage has already come
events.englishGloss: This is a case when he makes the stipulation, to either the east or the west, and the joining takes effect in that direction. He makes a stipulation because he does not know where the Sage came. The joining takes effect without the principle of retroactive designation. Nevertheless, since it is clear from the first case of Ayo that Rabbi Yehuda does not accept the principle of retroactive designation, the question remains: From where is it derived that an animal that is slaughtered on Shabbat or Yom Kippur is forbidden for the day that it was slaughtered?

events.hebrew: וְנוֹקְמַהּ בְּמֵזִיד, וְרַבִּי מֵאִיר!
events.englishLiteral: And let us interpret to intentionally, and Rabbi Meir,
events.englishGloss: The Gemara challenges this: the mishna as referring a case where he slaughtered the animal explain that it is in accordance with the opinion of who rules that eating the animal in such a case is permitted only after the conclusion of Shabbat.

events.hebrew: מַתְנִי׳ הַשּׁוֹחֵט בְּמַגַּל יָד, בְּצוֹר, וּבְקָנֶה – שְׁחִיטָתוֹ כְּשֵׁרָה.
events.englishLiteral: MISHNA: one who slaughters with a hand sickle, with flint, or with a reed his slaughter is valid.

events.hebrew: הַכֹּל שׁוֹחֲטִין, וּלְעוֹלָם שׁוֹחֲטִין, וּבַכֹּל שׁוֹחֲטִין, חוּץ מִמַּגַּל קָצִיר, וְהַמְּגֵירָה, וְהַשִּׁינַּיִם, וְהַצִּיפּוֹרֶן, מִפְּנֵי שֶׁהֵם חוֹנְקִין.
events.englishLiteral: All slaughter [ hakkol shoḥatin ], and one may always slaughter, and one may slaughter with any except for the harvest sickle, a saw, the teeth and a fingernail, because they strangle

events.hebrew: תָּנֵי תַּנָּא קַמֵּיהּ דְּרַב: הַמְבַשֵּׁל בְּשַׁבָּת, בְּשׁוֹגֵג – יֵאָכֵל, בְּמֵזִיד – לֹא יֵאָכֵל, וּמְשַׁתֵּיק לֵיהּ רַב.
events.englishLiteral: The tanna taught before Rav: One who cooks on Shabbat unwittingly may eat intentionally, he may not eat and Rav silenced him.

events.hebrew: מַאי טַעְמָא מְשַׁתֵּיק לֵיהּ? אִילֵּימָא מִשּׁוּם דִּסְבִירָא לֵיהּ כְּרַבִּי יְהוּדָה, וְתַנָּא תָּנֵי כְּרַבִּי מֵאִיר, מִשּׁוּם דִּסְבִירָא לֵיהּ כְּרַבִּי יְהוּדָה, מַאן דְּתָנֵי כְּרַבִּי מֵאִיר מְשַׁתֵּיק לֵיהּ?!
events.englishLiteral: What is the reason Rav silenced him? If we say because holds in accordance with Rabbi Yehuda and the tanna taught in accordance with Rabbi Meir, because he holds in accordance with Rabbi Yehuda he silences one who teaches in accordance with Rabbi Meir?

events.hebrew: וְעוֹד, מִי סָבַר לַהּ כְּרַבִּי יְהוּדָה? וְהָאָמַר רַב חָנָן בַּר אַמֵּי: כִּי מוֹרִי לְהוּ רַב לְתַלְמִידֵיהּ – מוֹרֵי לְהוּ כְּרַבִּי מֵאִיר, וְכִי דָּרֵישׁ בְּפִירְקָא – דָּרֵישׁ כְּרַבִּי יְהוּדָה, מִשּׁוּם עַמֵּי הָאָרֶץ!
events.englishLiteral: And furthermore, does hold in accordance with Rabbi Yehuda? But doesn’t Rav Ḥanan bar Ami say: When Rav issues a ruling to his students, he issues a ruling in accordance with Rabbi Meir, and when he teaches in public lecture he teaches in accordance with Rabbi Yehuda, due to the ignoramuses

events.hebrew: וְכִי תֵּימָא: תַּנָּא בְּפִירְקֵיהּ תְּנָא קַמֵּיהּ, אַטּוּ כּוּלֵּי עָלְמָא לְתַנָּא צָיְיתִי? לְאָמוֹרָא צָיְיתִי!
events.englishLiteral: And if you would say the tanna taught before during the public lecture is that to say everyone listens to the tanna they listen to the disseminator [ amora ],

There is no need to silence the tanna , because the Sage who repeats what he hears from Rav loudly for the benefit of those attending the lecture, and the amora quoted Rav’s ruling in accordance with the opinion of Rabbi Yehuda.

events.hebrew: אָמַר רַב נַחְמָן בַּר יִצְחָק: תַּנָּא שׁוֹחֵט תְּנָא קַמֵּיהּ דְּרַב: הַשּׁוֹחֵט בְּשַׁבָּת, בְּשׁוֹגֵג – יֵאָכֵל, בְּמֵזִיד – לֹא יֵאָכֵל. אֲמַר לֵיהּ: מַאי דַּעְתָּיךְ כְּרַבִּי מֵאִיר? עַד כָּאן לָא קָשָׁרֵי רַבִּי מֵאִיר אֶלָּא בִּמְבַשֵּׁל, דְּרָאוּי לָכוֹס, אֲבָל שׁוֹחֵט דְּאֵין רָאוּי לָכוֹס – לָא!

events.hebrew: כִּי שָׁרֵי רַבִּי מֵאִיר,
events.englishLiteral: When Rabbi Meir permits

events.hebrew: וְהָא מַתְנִיתִין דְּשׁוֹחֵט הוּא, וְאָמַר רַב הוּנָא: דָּרֵשׁ חִיָּיא בַּר רַב מִשְּׁמֵיהּ דְּרַב: אֲסוּרָה בַּאֲכִילָה לְיוֹמָא, וְנָסְבִין חַבְרַיָּא לְמֵימֵר: רַבִּי יְהוּדָה הִיא, הָא רַבִּי מֵאִיר שָׁרֵי!

events.hebrew: כְּגוֹן שֶׁהָיָה לוֹ חוֹלֶה מִבְּעוֹד יוֹם.
events.englishLiteral: where one had ill while it was still day,
events.englishGloss: it is in a case a critically person in his household before Shabbat, as it is permitted to slaughter the animal for such a person even on Shabbat. Therefore, the unslaughtered animal was not set aside from use.

events.hebrew: אִי הָכִי, מַאי טַעְמָא דְּרַבִּי יְהוּדָה דְּאָסַר? כְּגוֹן שֶׁהָיָה לוֹ חוֹלֶה וְהִבְרִיא.
events.englishLiteral: If so, what is the reason that Rabbi Yehuda prohibited where one had ill and recovered.
events.englishGloss: The Gemara asks: consumption of the meat on Shabbat? The Gemara answers: He issued this ruling in a case a critically person in his household before Shabbat that person In that case, although the unslaughtered animal was not set aside from use when Shabbat began, it is prohibited to slaughter it on Shabbat. According to Rabbi Yehuda, if he slaughtered it unwittingly, its consumption is prohibited on Shabbat.

events.hebrew: וְכִי הָא דְּאָמַר רַב אַחָא בַּר אַדָּא אָמַר רַב, וְאָמְרִי לַהּ אָמַר רַבִּי יִצְחָק בַּר אַדָּא אָמַר רַב: הַשּׁוֹחֵט לַחוֹלֶה בְּשַׁבָּת – אָסוּר לַבָּרִיא, הַמְבַשֵּׁל לַחוֹלֶה בְּשַׁבָּת – מוּתָּר לַבָּרִיא.

events.englishLiteral: to the matter of rendering seeds susceptible is tanna’im , as we learned one who places a bowl on the wall so that will be rinsed that is under when is placed so that the wall will not be damaged, it is not under when is placed
events.englishGloss: With regard to ritual impurity, there a dispute between in a mishna ( Makhshirin 4:3): In the case of while it is raining the bowl with the rainwater, if the water from the bowl then falls onto produce, the rubric of the verse: “But water upon the seed” (Leviticus 11:38). The water has the halakhic status of a liquid that he poured of his own volition on fruit and seeds. Consequently, it renders them susceptible to ritual impurity. But if he placed the bowl there the rubric of the verse “but water upon the seed.” Since he had no intent to use the water, it is not considered to have entered the bowl of his own volition, and it does not render produce susceptible to impurity.

events.hebrew: תָּא שְׁמַע: נָעַץ סַכִּין בַּכּוֹתֶל וְשָׁחַט בָּהּ – שְׁחִיטָתוֹ כְּשֵׁרָה. שָׁאנֵי סַכִּין, דְּלָא מְבַטֵּל לֵיהּ.

events.hebrew: הָא גּוּפַהּ קַשְׁיָא: אָמְרַתְּ, בִּשְׁבִיל שֶׁתּוּדַח – הֲרֵי זֶה בְּ״כִי יוּתַּן״, הָא בִּשְׁבִיל שֶׁיּוּדַח הַכּוֹתֶל – אֵין זֶה בְּ״כִי יוּתַּן״.
events.englishLiteral: This itself difficult, you said: so that will be rinsed that is under when is placed so that the wall will be rinsed that is not under when is placed
events.englishGloss: mishna is as the inferences from the first clause and the latter clause are contradictory. In the first clause In the case of one who places a bowl on the wall the bowl with the rainwater, the rubric of the verse “but water upon the seed,” and the water renders produce susceptible to impurity. By inference, if he placed the bowl by means of the bowl, the rubric of the verse “but water upon the seed.” That water would not render produce susceptible to impurity, because the intent was for the water to rinse the wall, which is an item attached to the ground.

events.hebrew: וַהֲדַר תָּנֵי: בִּשְׁבִיל שֶׁלֹּא יִלְקֶה הַכּוֹתֶל – אֵינוֹ בְּ״כִי יוּתַּן״, הָא בִּשְׁבִיל שֶׁיּוּדַח הַכּוֹתֶל – הֲרֵי זֶה בְּ״כִי יוּתַּן״.

events.hebrew: אֵימַת? אִילֵּימָא בְּשֶׁבַע שֶׁכִּבְּשׁוּ – הַשְׁתָּא דָּבָר טָמֵא אִישְׁתְּרִי לְהוּ, דִּכְתִיב: ״וּבָתִּים מְלֵאִים כׇּל טוּב״, וְאָמַר רַבִּי יִרְמְיָה בַּר אַבָּא אָמַר רַב: כֻּתְלֵי דַּחֲזִירֵי – בְּשַׂר נְחִירָה מִבַּעְיָא?
events.englishLiteral: When? If we say with regard to seven which they conquered now, non-kosher items were permitted for them as it is written: and houses full of all good things… and Rabbi Yirmeya bar Abba says Rav says: Cuts of pig [ kotlei daḥazirei ] necessary meat stabbing
events.englishGloss: The Gemara asks: With regard to what period does Rabbi Yirmeya raise his dilemma? that the dilemma is the years during the land, during that period, “And it shall be, when the Lord your God shall bring you into the land that He swore to your fathers, and you shall eat and be satisfied” (Deuteronomy 6:10–11), that meat that they found in the houses were permitted for them; is it to say that the from the of a kosher animal was permitted?

events.hebrew: אֶלָּא, לְאַחַר מִכָּאן. וְאִיבָּעֵית אֵימָא: לְעוֹלָם בְּשֶׁבַע שֶׁכִּבְּשׁוּ, כִּי אִשְׁתְּרִי לְהוּ שָׁלָל שֶׁל גּוֹיִם – דִּידְהוּ לָא אִישְׁתְּרִי. תֵּיקוּ.

events.hebrew: אָמַר רַבָּה: שַׁנֵּית ״הַכֹּל שׁוֹחֲטִין״ וּ״לְעוֹלָם שׁוֹחֲטִין״, ״בַּכֹּל שׁוֹחֲטִים״ – מַאי מְשַׁנֵּית לֵיהּ?
events.englishLiteral: Rabba says: You explained All slaughter, and: One may always slaughter. what do you explain One may slaughter with any

events.hebrew: וְכִי תֵּימָא, בֵּין בְּצוֹר בֵּין בִּזְכוּכִית בֵּין בִּקְרוּמִית שֶׁל קָנֶה, הָא דּוּמְיָא דְּהָנָךְ קָתָנֵי; אִי הָנָךְ בְּשׁוֹחֲטִין – הַאי נָמֵי בְּשׁוֹחֲטִין, וְאִי הָנָךְ בְּנִשְׁחָטִין – הַאי נָמֵי בְּנִשְׁחָטִין.
events.englishLiteral: And if you would say Whether with a flint, or with glass or with the stalk of a reed, but isn’t taught similar to those If these to those that slaughter, this too to those that slaughter; and if those to those that are slaughtered, this too to those that are slaughtered.
events.englishGloss: that it means: shards, this phrase in a manner other phrases in the mishna? phrases: All slaughter, and: One may always slaughter, are referring phrase is referring phrases are referring phrase is referring The first two phrases in the mishna were explained as referring to the animals that are slaughtered. The first phrase was interpreted to include birds, and the second phrase was interpreted as referring to the halakha that meat may be eaten only through slaughter of the animal.

events.hebrew: אֶלָּא אָמַר רָבָא: ״הַכֹּל שׁוֹחֲטִין״, חֲדָא לְאֵתוֹיֵי כּוּתִי, וַחֲדָא לְאֵתוֹיֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל מְשׁוּמָּד. ״לְעוֹלָם שׁוֹחֲטִין״ – בֵּין בַּיּוֹם בֵּין בַּלַּיְלָה, בֵּין בְּרֹאשׁ הַגָּג בֵּין בְּרֹאשׁ הַסְּפִינָה. ״בַּכֹּל שׁוֹחֲטִין״ – בֵּין בְּצוֹר, בֵּין בִּזְכוּכִית, בֵּין בִּקְרוּמִית שֶׁל קָנֶה.
events.englishLiteral: Rather, Rava said everyone [ hakkol ] slaughters. One is to include a Samaritan and one is to include a Jewish transgressor. One may always slaughter, both during the day and at night, both on a rooftop and atop a ship, One may slaughter with any Whether with a flint, or with glass or with the stalk of a reed.
events.englishGloss: that the entire mishna is referring to those that slaughter. The initial phrase means Although an identical phrase was used in the first mishna (2a), both are necessary: The second phrase: means and there is no concern that it will appear that he is slaughtering in an idolatrous manner to the hosts of heaven or to the god of the sea. The phrase: item that cuts, means: shards,

events.hebrew: חוּץ מִמַּגַּל קָצִיר וְהַמְּגֵירָה. אֲבוּהּ דִּשְׁמוּאֵל פְּגַם וְשַׁדַּר, פְּגַם וְשַׁדַּר. שְׁלַחוּ לֵיהּ: כִּמְגֵירָה שָׁנִינוּ.
events.englishLiteral: Except for the harvest sickle, and the saw. Shmuel’s father notch and send notch and send They sent to him We learned like a saw,

events.hebrew: תָּנוּ רַבָּנַן:
events.englishLiteral: The Sages taught
events.englishGloss: in a baraita :

events.hebrew: סַכִּין שֶׁיֵּשׁ בָּהּ פְּגִימוֹת הַרְבֵּה – תִּידּוֹן כִּמְגֵירָה, וְשֶׁאֵין בָּהּ אֶלָּא פְּגִימָה אַחַת: אוֹגֶרֶת – פְּסוּלָה, מְסוּכְסֶכֶת – כְּשֵׁרָה. הֵיכִי דָּמְיָא אוֹגֶרֶת? הֵיכִי דָּמְיָא מְסוּכְסֶכֶת? אָמַר רַבִּי אֱלִיעֶזֶר: אוֹגֶרֶת – מִשְׁתֵּי רוּחוֹת, מְסוּכְסֶכֶת – מֵרוּחַ אַחַת.
events.englishLiteral: a knife in which there are several notches is considered like a saw; and in which there is only one notch, catches, unfit, entangles [ mesukhsekhet ], fit. What are the circumstances catches, what are the circumstances entangles? Rabbi Eliezer said: catches on two sides, entangles on one side.

events.hebrew: מַאי שְׁנָא מִשְׁתֵּי רוּחוֹת, דְּמוּרְשָׁא קַמָּא מַחְלֵישׁ וּמוּרְשָׁא בָּתְרָא בָּזַע? מֵרוּחַ אַחַת נָמֵי, חוּרְפָּא דְּסַכִּינָא מַחְלֵישׁ, מוּרְשָׁא בָּזַע! דְּקָאֵים אַרֵישָׁא דְּסַכִּינָא. סוֹף סוֹף, כִּי אָזְלָא מַחְלְשָׁא, כִּי (אתא בזע) [אָתְיָא בָּזְעָה]! כְּגוֹן שֶׁהוֹלִיךְ וְלֹא הֵבִיא.
events.englishLiteral: What is different on two sides, where the first edge [ moresha ] compromises and the latter edge rips on one side too, the sharp of the knife compromises the edge rips that stands at the top of the knife, Ultimately, when goes it compromises when it comes it rips where he drew the knife backward and did not draw it forward.
events.englishGloss: The Gemara challenges this explanation: about a notch with a sharp edge the neck by removing the hide and the flesh, the simanim ; in the case of a notch with a sharp edge tip the neck and of the notch the simanim . The Gemara explains: The reference is to a notch which begins the slaughter. The Gemara objects: the knife in one direction the neck and back in the other direction the simanim . The Gemara explains: The reference is to a case

events.hebrew: אָמַר רָבָא: שָׁלֹשׁ מִדּוֹת בְּסַכִּין – אוֹגֶרֶת – לֹא יִשְׁחוֹט, וְאִם שָׁחַט – שְׁחִיטָתוֹ פְּסוּלָה. מְסוּכְסֶכֶת – לֹא יִשְׁחוֹט בָּהּ לְכִתְחִלָּה, וְאִם שָׁחַט – שְׁחִיטָתוֹ כְּשֵׁרָה. עוֹלֶה וְיוֹרֵד בְּסַכִּין – שׁוֹחֵט בָּהּ לְכַתְּחִלָּה.
events.englishLiteral: Rava says: three types in a knife. catches, one may not slaughter and if he slaughtered, his slaughter is not valid. entangles, one may not slaughter with it ab initio ; and if he slaughtered his slaughter is valid rises and falls in knife one may slaughter with it ab initio .
events.englishGloss: There are of notches If the notch with it, If the notch with it, after the fact. If the notch the and has no sharp edges,

events.hebrew: אֲמַר לֵיהּ רַב הוּנָא בְּרֵיהּ דְּרַב נְחֶמְיָה לְרַב אָשֵׁי: אֲמַרְתְּ לַן מִשְּׁמֵיהּ דְּרָבָא: מְסוּכְסֶכֶת פְּסוּלָה, וְהָא אָמַר רָבָא: מְסוּכְסֶכֶת כְּשֵׁרָה! לָא קַשְׁיָא: כָּאן שֶׁהוֹלִיךְ וְהֵבִיא, כָּאן שֶׁהוֹלִיךְ וְלֹא הֵבִיא.
events.englishLiteral: Rav Huna, son of Rav Neḥemya, said to Rav Ashi: You said to us in the name of Rava entangles, not valid. But doesn’t Rava say: entangles, valid? not difficult. Here, where he drew the knife back and forth. There, where he drew the knife backward and did not draw it forward.
events.englishGloss: that if the notch the slaughter is If the notch the slaughter is Rav Ashi answers: This is where Rava says that the slaughter is not valid, is in a case where Rava says that the slaughter is valid, is in a case

events.hebrew: אֲמַר לֵיהּ רַב אַחָא בְּרֵיהּ דְּרַב אַוְיָא לְרַב אָשֵׁי: דָּמְיָא לְסָאסָא, מַאי? אֲמַר לֵיהּ: מַאן יָהֵיב לַן מִבִּשְׂרֵיהּ וְאָכְלִינַן.
events.englishLiteral: Rav Aḥa, son of Rav Avya, said to Rav Ashi: similar to an awn [ sasa ], what said to him: Who will give us from the meat of and we will eat
events.englishGloss: If the knife was of grain which is not perfectly smooth but does not have actual notches, is the halakha ? Rav Ashi an animal slaughtered with that knife, it.

events.hebrew: אָמַר רַב חִסְדָּא: מִנַּיִן לִבְדִיקַת סַכִּין מִן הַתּוֹרָה? שֶׁנֶּאֱמַר: ״וּשְׁחַטְתֶּם בָּזֶה וַאֲכַלְתֶּם״.
events.englishLiteral: Rav Ḥisda says: From where that examination of a knife by Torah law? as it is stated “And slaughter with this and eat”
events.englishGloss: § is it derived is an obligation It is derived from a verse, with regard to Saul’s instructions to the people: (I Samuel 14:34), indicating that Saul gave them the knife only after ensuring that it was fit to slaughter their animals.

events.hebrew: פְּשִׁיטָא, כֵּיוָן דְּכִי נְקַב טְרֵיפָה, בָּעֲיָא בְּדִיקָה לְחָכָם קָאָמְרִינַן! וְהָאָמַר רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן: לֹא אָמְרוּ לְהַרְאוֹת סַכִּין לְחָכָם אֶלָּא מִפְּנֵי כְּבוֹדוֹ שֶׁל חָכָם! מִדְּרַבָּנַן, וּקְרָא אַסְמַכְתָּא בְּעָלְמָא הוּא.

events.englishGloss: The Gemara asks: Isn’t it that a knife must be examined before slaughter? in the gullet, the animal would be a therefore the knife to prevent that situation. The Gemara answers: that a source for the halakha that one must show the knife a Torah for examination is needed. The Gemara asks: Is that an obligation by Torah law? that the Sages the a Torah the requirement to show the Torah The Gemara answers: Indeed, it is a requirement cited as for that practice, not as a source.

events.hebrew: בְּמַעְרְבָא בָּדְקִי לַהּ בְּשִׁימְשָׁא, בִּנְהַרְדְּעָא בָּדְקוּ לַהּ בְּמַיָּא, רַב שֵׁשֶׁת בָּדֵק לַהּ בְּרֵישׁ לִישָּׁנֵיהּ, רַב אַחָא בַּר יַעֲקֹב בָּדֵק לַהּ בְּחוּט הַשַּׂעֲרָה.
events.englishLiteral: In the West, they examine in the sun In Neharde’a they examine with water. Rav Sheshet would examine it with the tip of his tongue. Rav Aḥa bar Ya’akov would examine it with a strand of hair.
events.englishGloss: The Gemara notes: Eretz Yisrael, the knife to determine whether there is a notch. the knife They would place the blade on the surface of the water, and if there was a notch, it would noticeably alter the surface of the water. He would pass the strand over the blade of the knife and if there was a notch, it would be caught in that notch.

events.hebrew: בְּסוּרָא אָמְרִי: בִּישְׂרָא אָכְלָה, בִּישְׂרָא לִבְדְּקַהּ. אָמַר רַב פָּפָּא: צְרִיכָא בְּדִיקָה אַבִּישְׂרָא, וְאַטּוּפְרָא, וְאַתְּלָתָא רוּחָתָא.
events.englishLiteral: In Sura they say: consumes the flesh; let the flesh examine Rav Pappa said: requires examination on the flesh, and on the fingernail, and on the three sides
events.englishGloss: The knife the knife. Since the concern is that the knife will rip the flesh during the slaughter, it should be examined by passing it on the tongue or the fingertip. The knife of the knife, i.e., the blade and the two sides of the knife.

events.hebrew: אֲמַר לֵיהּ רָבִינָא לְרַב אָשֵׁי: אָמַר לַן רַב סַמָּא בְּרֵיהּ דְּרַב מְשַׁרְשְׁיָא מִשְּׁמָךְ, דַּאֲמַרְתְּ לֵיהּ מִשְּׁמֵיהּ דְּרָבָא: צְרִיכָא בְּדִיקָה אַבִּישְׂרָא וְאַטּוּפְרָא וְאַתְּלָתָא רוּחָתָא. אֲמַר לֵיהּ: אַבִּישְׂרָא וְאַטּוּפְרָא אֲמַרִי, וְאַתְּלָתָא רוּחָתָא לָא אֲמַרִי. אִיכָּא דְּאָמְרִי: אַבִּישְׂרָא וְאַטּוּפְרָא וְאַתְּלָתָא רוּחָתָא אֲמַרִי, מִשְּׁמֵיהּ דְּרָבָא לָא אֲמַרִי.
events.englishLiteral: Ravina said to Rav Ashi: Rav Sama, son of Rav Mesharshiyya, said to us in your name that you said to him in the name of Rava: requires examination on the flesh, and on the fingernail, and on the three sides. said to On the flesh and on the fingernail I said, and on the three sides I did not say. There are who say On the flesh and on the fingernail and on the three sides I said, in the name of Rava I did not say.
events.englishGloss: which The knife Rav Ashi Ravina: those that Rav Ashi said to him: and

events.hebrew: רָבִינָא וְרַב אַחָא בְּרֵיהּ דְּרָבָא הֲווֹ יָתְבִי קַמֵּיהּ דְּרַב אָשֵׁי, אַיְיתוֹ סַכִּין לְקַמֵּיהּ דְּרַב אָשֵׁי לִבְדְּקַהּ. אֲמַר לֵיהּ לְרַב אַחָא בְּרֵיהּ דְּרָבָא: בִּידְקַהּ. בַּדְקַהּ אַטּוּפְרָא וְאַבִּישְׂרָא וְאַתְּלָתָא רוּחָתָא. אֲמַר לֵיהּ: יִישַׁר, וְכֵן אָמַר רַב כָּהֲנָא.
events.englishLiteral: Ravina and Rav Aḥa, son of Rava, were sitting before Rav Ashi. brought a knife before Rav Ashi to examine it. said to Rav Aḥa, son of Rava: Examine it. He examined it on the fingernail, and on the flesh, and on the three sides. said to him: Well done, and Rav Kahana likewise said
events.englishGloss: People Rav Ashi Rav Ashi that this is the way to examine a knife.

events.hebrew: בַּדְקֵהּ רַב אָשֵׁי לְסַכִּינֵיהּ, וְנִמְצֵאת יָפָה, וְאַכְשְׁרֵיהּ. אֲמַר לֵיהּ מָר זוּטְרָא: וְלָא לֵיחוּשׁ מָר לְסָבָא? אֲמַר לֵיהּ: שְׁלִיחוּתֵיהּ קָא עָבְדִינַן.

The Gemara answers: that the reference is the cutting of siman as one is required to cut both simanim in a bird ab initio . instead that the reference is the passage in the mishna that states that the halakhic status of siman that of the entire siman , as one is required to cut the entire siman ab initio .

events.hebrew: אֲמַר לֵיהּ: בַּעְלָהּ חוּלִּין הוּא, וְחוּלִּין לָא בָּעֵי כַּוָּונָה. וּמְנָא תֵּימְרָא? דִּתְנַן: גַּל שֶׁנִּתְלַשׁ וּבוֹ אַרְבָּעִים סְאָה וְנָפַל עַל הָאָדָם וְעַל הַכֵּלִים – טְהוֹרִין. מַאי לָאו אָדָם דּוּמְיָא דְּכֵלִים? מָה כֵּלִים דְּלָא מִיכַּוְּונִי, אַף אָדָם נָמֵי לָא בָּעֵי כַּוָּונָה.

events.englishLiteral: In what is this statement said? when one drew back and did not draw forth, Rav Menashe said: in a scalpel that does not have protrusions
events.englishGloss: § The mishna continues: case It is the knife it or drew it forth and did not draw it back. But if he drew it back and forth, even if the knife was of any length, even if he slaughtered with a scalpel, the slaughter is valid. This is the halakha the case of from the sides. If there are protrusions, since the scalpel is short, there is concern that the corners may perforate the simanim or enter between the simanim and invalidate the slaughter.

events.englishLiteral: Rav Pappa said: I was sitting before Rav Aḥa bar Yaakov and I sought to say to him Is there anything that is permitted for a Jew but prohibited for a gentile? But I did not say to him, as I said Didn’t he say a reason
events.englishGloss: that his statement is difficult: that to myself: for his ruling? Therefore, there is no reason to ask the question.

events.cueText: the ability to be makabal on himself to eat chulin in Taraset Kodesh. It's a meaningless statement. If a person says that he's going to eat Shruma Al Taraset Kodesh, it's dvarim betayim. It doesn't accomplish anything, Rashi says.

events.hebrew: טוּמְאָה הִיא אֵצֶל הֶקְדֵּשׁ.
events.englishLiteral: is impurity vis-à-vis sacrificial
events.englishGloss: food. The Sages issued a decree that even one who is vigilant to partake of his teruma in a state of ritual purity is considered impure vis-à-vis one partaking of sacrificial food. Therefore, one who ate a food item that was prepared on the level of purity of teruma that had become impure with third-degree impurity assumes second-degree impurity, and he renders sacrificial food impure.

events.hebrew: אֲמַר לֵיהּ: לָא מִיבַּעְיָא קָאָמַר.

Rabbi Yehoshua teaches that non-sacred food items that were prepared on the level of purity of teruma also have the capacity of assuming third-degree impurity.

In that case too, the Rabbis should have said: Once the animal convulses with its foreleg or with its hind leg or it wags its tail. Apparently, blood spurting is a less substantive indication of life, and the proof of Rav Naḥman bar Yitzḥak remains valid: If spurting at the beginning of the act of slaughter is a sufficient indication of life to render the slaughter valid, then all the more so, extending and bending a limb are also sufficient indications of life.

events.englishLiteral: GEMARA: unfit, yes; offerings to the dead, not And raises a contradiction one who slaughters for the sake of mountains, for the sake of hills, for the sake of rivers, for the sake of wildernesses, for the sake of the sun and moon, for the sake of stars and constellations, for the sake of Michael the great ministering angel, for the sake of a small worm, these are offerings to the dead,
events.englishGloss: The mishna states that if one slaughters for the sake of mountains or other natural entities the slaughter is unfit. The Gemara infers: It is with regard to i.e., to idols, it is in that category. Apparently, the status of the animal is that of an unslaughtered carcass, from which benefit is permitted, and not that of an idolatrous offering, from which benefit is forbidden. the Gemara from a baraita : With regard to or even from which benefit is forbidden.

events.hebrew: אָמַר אַבָּיֵי: לָא קַשְׁיָא, הָא דְּאָמַר לְהַר, הָא דְּאָמַר לְגַדָּא דְּהַר. דַּיְקָא נָמֵי, דְּקָתָנֵי דּוּמְיָא דְּמִיכָאֵל שַׂר הַגָּדוֹל, שְׁמַע מִינַּהּ.
events.englishLiteral: Abaye said: not difficult. This where one says of the mountain That where one says of the angel of the mountain. is also precise, as are taught similar to Michael, the great ministering angel. Conclude from it
events.englishGloss: The apparent contradiction between the mishna and the baraita is mishna that teaches that the slaughter is not valid but benefit is permitted is referring to a case that he is slaughtering the animal for the sake itself, which is not an idol. baraita that teaches that the animal is an offering to the dead and benefit is forbidden is referring to a case that he is slaughtering the animal for the sake The language of the baraita the mountain and the other natural entities together with and therefore that the tanna is referring to slaughter for the sake of a spiritual entity, not the mountain itself.

events.hebrew: אֶלָּא, בְּאוֹמֵר בִּגְמַר זְבִיחָה הוּא עוֹבְדָהּ.
events.englishLiteral: Rather, where one says he is worshipping at the conclusion of the slaughter;
events.englishGloss: The Gemara answers: the baraita is referring to a case prior to the slaughter that the idol only therefore, only then is the animal rendered forbidden, and one is liable for all three sin offerings simultaneously.

events.hebrew: אָמַר רַב הוּנָא: הָיְתָה בֶּהֱמַת חֲבֵירוֹ רְבוּצָה לִפְנֵי עֲבוֹדָה זָרָה, כֵּיוָן שֶׁשָּׁחַט בָּהּ סִימָן אֶחָד – אֲסָרָהּ. סָבַר לַהּ כִּי הָא דְּאָמַר עוּלָּא אָמַר רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן: אַף עַל פִּי שֶׁאָמְרוּ הַמִּשְׁתַּחֲוֶה לְבֶהֱמַת חֲבֵירוֹ לֹא אֲסָרָהּ, עָשָׂה בָּהּ מַעֲשֶׂה – אֲסָרָהּ.
events.englishLiteral: Rav Huna says: If the animal of another was prone before an idol, once one cut one siman , he rendered forbidden. He holds in accordance with that which Ulla says Rabbi Yoḥanan says: Although said one who bows to the animal of another does not render it forbidden, he performed rite upon it he renders it forbidden.
events.englishGloss: the windpipe or the gullet, the animal that the Sages that if a sacrificial The case cited by Rav Huna involves an action of that kind, cutting one siman ; therefore, the animal is forbidden.

events.hebrew: אֵיתִיבֵיהּ רַב נַחְמָן לְרַב הוּנָא: הַשּׁוֹחֵט חַטָּאת בְּשַׁבָּת בַּחוּץ לַעֲבוֹדָה זָרָה – חַיָּיב שָׁלֹשׁ חַטָּאוֹת, וְאִי אָמְרַתְּ: כֵּיוָן שֶׁשָּׁחַט בָּהּ סִימָן אֶחָד אֲסָרָהּ – אַשְּׁחוּטֵי חוּץ לָא לִיחַיַּיב,
events.englishLiteral: Rav Naḥman raised an objection to Rav Huna One who slaughters a sin offering on Shabbat outside for idol worship, is liable three sin offerings: And if you say once he cuts one siman he renders forbidden let him not be liable for slaughter outside
events.englishGloss: the opinion of from a baraita : unwittingly an animal that was designated as the Temple, to bring One for performing the prohibited labor of slaughtering on Shabbat, one for slaughtering a sacrificial animal outside the Temple, and one for slaughtering an animal for idol worship. that the animal as an idolatrous offering, then to bring a sin offering of a sacrificial animal the Temple courtyard,

events.hebrew: מְחַתֵּךְ בְּעָפָר הוּא! אָמַר רַב פָּפָּא: הָכָא בְּחַטַּאת הָעוֹף עָסְקִינַן, דְּכוּלְּהוּ בַּהֲדֵי הֲדָדֵי קָאָתֵי.
events.englishLiteral: he is chopping in dirt, Rav Pappa said: Here we are dealing with a bird sin offering, all of come simultaneously.
events.englishGloss: as it is as though merely since one is not liable for slaughtering outside the Temple courtyard a sacrificial animal unfit for sacrifice. for which the requirement is to cut only one siman , and when cutting that siman , the three prohibitions to be violated

events.hebrew: מִכְּדֵי רַב הוּנָא כְּמַאן אַמְרַהּ לִשְׁמַעְתֵּיהּ? כְּעוּלָּא, וְעוּלָּא מַעֲשֶׂה כֹּל דְּהוּ קָאָמַר.
events.englishLiteral: Now in accordance with whose Rav Huna state his halakha ? of Ulla, And Ulla says a minimal action
events.englishGloss: The Gemara asks: opinion did It is in accordance with the opinion who says: If he performed a sacrificial rite upon the animal, he renders it forbidden. that renders the animal forbidden, as his ruling applies even to cutting one siman . According to Ulla’s opinion, the moment that he begins the incision, the animal is forbidden and unfit to be sacrificed. Consequently, when he completes the slaughter outside the Temple, it is as though he is chopping dirt. Why then is he liable to bring a sin offering for slaughter of a sacrificial animal outside the Temple courtyard?
